title: How Many Colleges Should I Actually Apply To?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You see videos of students applying to 20, 30, even 40 colleges. They're stressed. They're exhausted. Their parents are broke from application fees. Here's the truth: applying to more schools does not increase your chances of admission proportionally. There is a point of diminishing returns. Here is the math of the perfect application list.

The Three Categories

Your college list should have three types of schools: safety, target, and reach.

Safety schools: Your GPA and test scores are above the 75th percentile of admitted students. You are almost certain to get in. These schools should be affordable (in-state public, guaranteed merit scholarships, or schools where you'd be happy to attend). Aim for 2-3 safeties.

Target schools: Your GPA and test scores are in the middle 50% range of admitted students. You have a realistic chance (30-70%). These are where you'll likely end up. Aim for 4-6 targets.

Reach schools: Your GPA and test scores are below the 25th percentile of admitted students, or the school has a very low acceptance rate (under 20%). You might get in, but you shouldn't count on it. Aim for 2-4 reaches.

Total: 8-13 schools. That's enough. More than 15 is usually unnecessary.

The Application Fee Math

The average application fee is $50-$75. Some are higher. If you apply to 20 schools, you're spending $1,000-$1,500 just on applications. That's before you pay for score sends, transcript fees, and travel for visits.

Fee waivers exist for low-income students. Use them if you qualify. But even with waivers, the time cost is real. Each application takes hours. Those hours could be spent improving your grades or doing meaningful extracurriculars.

The Supplemental Essay Trap

The Common App has one main essay. Then each school adds supplements. 'Why us?' essays. Short answers. Optional statements. These are not optional if you want to be competitive.

Applying to 20 schools means writing 20 'Why us?' essays. The quality will drop. Generic essays don't impress admissions officers. Better to write 8 excellent supplements than 20 mediocre ones.

The Balanced List Formula

Here is a practical list for most students:

• 2 safety schools (one you love, one you like)
• 4 target schools (2 where you're slightly above average, 2 where you're average)
• 2 reach schools (your dream schools, plus one more long shot)

If you're a strong candidate (top 10% of your class, high test scores), you can add one more reach. If you're worried about admission, add one more safety.

This is 8-9 schools. Manageable. You can write quality essays. You won't burn out.

Early Decision Changes the Math

If you apply Early Decision to your top choice (binding), you can reduce your list. Once you're admitted ED, you withdraw all other applications. You don't need as many targets and reaches.

If you're rejected or deferred ED, you'll need your regular decision list. Don't skip safeties just because you're applying ED. Things don't always go as planned.

What About Tuition and Financial Aid?

Your list should include affordable options. In-state public schools are usually cheaper. Some private schools offer generous aid. Run the Net Price Calculator for each school before you apply. Don't fall in love with a school you can't afford.

Some students add 'financial safety' schools — schools where you're guaranteed merit aid based on your stats. These are worth including even if they're not your top choice.

You don't need to apply to 20 schools. You need to apply to the right schools. Research each one. Write genuine supplements. Visit if you can. Quality over quantity is not a cliché. It's a strategy.
